default_title: Open Source Strategy — IP Ownership & Licensing Decision
landing_intro: Should Meridian be open-sourced? The answer depends on a threshold question that hasn't been resolved: who owns the IP? This module distills the strategic analysis into the key decision points, stress-tested arguments on both sides, and a recommended posture that captures the upside without premature commitment. The strongest single line: the realistic downside of OS is obscurity, not theft; the realistic upside is survival beyond your tenure.
checklist_section_label: Open source readiness — confirm all four

checklist.statement: IP ownership question framed — if built on Optum time/equipment/workflows, Optum likely owns the IP; resolve before any OS decision
License model chosen — open-core: Apache 2.0 for framework, CC-BY-SA 4.0 for clinical content, Optum-specific modules private
Governance template drafted — GOVERNANCE.md with "maintainers have final say; roadmap owned by Dr. Schmuckler" contract
Timeline gated to 3 conditions — (a) Optum IP signoff, (b) first non-Optum pilot site, (c) stable module schema v2

green_zone.zone_label: Recommended posture: don't OS yet, declare intent with conditions
green_zone.narrative_html: The recommended posture is to not open-source yet — no buyers, no external contributors asking, no regulatory clarity, no IP resolution with Optum. Opening now is a whimper. Instead, publish on the site: "Will open-source the framework layer when we hit X" where X = Optum IP signoff + first non-Optum pilot + stable schema v2. This gives all the goodwill benefits with none of the premature commitment. Avoid AGPL — Bahmni's 2025 reversal (AGPL→MPL/MIT) is the current-year evidence.

escalation.statement: IP ownership unresolved — if Optum owns it, "should we OS?" is not your question to answer; it must go through Optum OSS governance (opensource.optum.com)
OSEHRA cautionary tale — 850+ members, collapsed in 2020 when the VA (anchor tenant) left for Cerner; single-sponsor OS in healthcare dies when the sponsor loses interest
Maintainer burnout risk — "Doctors Who Code" warns of stall-guilt-abandon arc; Linux Foundation 2024: 60% of maintainers unpaid
Optum strategic interest unclear — is Meridian (a) internal differentiator, (b) PR/recruiting asset, (c) category-defining platform? Answer dictates license
"Security through goodwill" is a myth — OS code is more attackable in discovery phase; Linus's Law only works with enough eyes, which most projects don't have
Copycat risk is real but asymmetric — defense is speed + clinical credibility, not secrecy; a physician-built product with a UC director's name is harder to replicate than code
Regulatory exposure grows with visibility — FDA non-device posture holds today, but widely-adopted public tool invites closer scrutiny
Competitive moat concern — OS gives trigger schema + UX patterns to Navina for free; counterargument: moat is clinical judgment + physician trust + Optum distribution, not code
Content scaling bottleneck — module roadmap bottlenecked on one physician; other physicians contributing modules is the only path to breadth
Avoid AGPL — Bahmni relicensed AGPL→MPL/MIT in Nov 2025 explicitly to grow adoption; AGPL costs more adoption than it buys in capture protection

context_strip.label: Current state
context_strip.text: Pre-enterprise, pre-Epic integration, solo builder. No IP resolution with Optum. No external contributors asking. GitHub repo exists (noahschmuckler/meridian) but is not positioned as an open-source project.

footer_note: Source: docs/OPEN_SOURCE_STRATEGY.md — April 2026. Full document includes Apache 2.0 landscape analysis, OSEHRA case study, and five philosophical decision questions for the group.

items.question: Why is IP ownership the threshold question?
items.answer_html:
If Meridian was built on Optum time, equipment, or against Optum/Kaiser workflows and data concepts, Optum likely owns the IP under your employment agreement. "Should we open-source?" may not be your question to answer — it's a question to bring to Optum's OSS governance.

Optum already runs opensource.optum.com and has a published contribution process. Treating this as a personal decision when it's actually a corporate one is the fastest way to lose control of the project entirely. Resolve this first.

Threshold question on GitHub

items.question: What does Optum's strategic interest look like?
items.answer_html:
Four possible framings — the answer dictates licensing:

A. Internal differentiator — Meridian gives Optum clinicians an edge. Keep closed.
B. PR/recruiting asset — one more Optum OS repo. Incremental value only unless positioned deliberately.
C. Category-defining platform — requires executive sponsorship and OS release.
D. Open-core hybrid — framework open, Optum-specific content private. Best of both.

faqs.title: What survives scrutiny on both sides?

items.question: Which pro-OS arguments hold up?
items.answer_html:
Trust is table stakes — auditable source is increasingly expected by health-system buyers. Metriport's thesis is that OSS wins enterprise healthcare deals.

Clinical content scales through contribution — the module roadmap is bottlenecked on one physician. Other physicians contributing modules is the only path to breadth. The DOCX→JSON pipeline makes this accessible to physicians who can write a Word document.

It forecloses internal-tool death — internal tools die when the builder changes roles. OS + external adopters = institutional resilience beyond your Optum tenure.

Standards alignment is free — building against CDS Hooks / SMART on FHIR in the open signals you're not a walled garden.

The strongest single line: The realistic downside of OS for Meridian is obscurity, not theft. The realistic upside is survival beyond your tenure.

Full arguments on GitHub

items.question: Which arguments don't survive?
items.answer_html:
"Security through goodwill" — this is a myth. OS code is more attackable in the discovery phase. OpenSSF 2025 warns of state-actor supply-chain attacks on OSS. Linus's Law only works with enough eyes, which most projects don't have. The real security story is BAA + audit backend + HITRUST.

"PR deluge" — unlikely. Almost no OS project has this problem. The far more common failure is crickets.

"Attracting top-tier dev talent" — partially real, but Meridian's technical surface is small (single-file HTML + clinical JSON). You'd attract clinical content contributors more than senior engineers — which may be more valuable, but is a different bet.

items.question: What is the open-core recommendation?
items.answer_html:
Apache 2.0 for the framework (rendering engine, module schema, CDS Hooks integration) — permissive, Optum-friendly, maximum adoption.

CC-BY-SA 4.0 for clinical content JSON — so clinical edits must flow back to the community.

Private for Optum-specific modules and payer rules.

This is the open-core pattern that actually works in healthcare. The framework is the growth engine; the institution-specific content is the business value.

items.question: Why avoid AGPL?
items.answer_html:
Bahmni relicensed AGPL→MPL/MIT in November 2025, explicitly to grow adoption. AGPL's copyleft requirement scared away enterprise adopters who couldn't (or wouldn't) comply. This is the current-year evidence that AGPL costs more adoption than it buys in capture protection.

Apache 2.0 is the modern default for healthcare interop/CDS: CDS Hooks, OpenCDS, and most FHIR tooling use it. AGPL is actively losing share in healthcare.

Licensing landscape on GitHub

faqs.title: What should we do right now?

items.question: What is the recommended posture?
items.answer_html:
Don't open-source yet. No buyers, no external contributors asking, no regulatory clarity, no IP resolution with Optum. Opening now is a whimper.

Instead, posture it as "will open-source the framework layer when we hit X." Published on the site. X = three conditions:

1. Optum IP signoff.
2. First non-Optum pilot site.
3. Stable module schema v2.

This gives all the goodwill benefits with none of the premature commitment.

Recommendation on GitHub

items.question: What are the five philosophical questions for the group?
items.answer_html:
1. Is Meridian a product or a framework? Products stay closed. Frameworks open up. Open-core splits the difference.

2. Is the clinical content a moat or a contribution surface? If the former, closed. If the latter, open is the only way to scale.

3. What's the 3-year horizon for your personal involvement? If you might move roles, OS is the only way it survives without you.

4. Does Optum want Meridian as (a) internal differentiator, (b) PR/recruiting asset, or (c) category-defining platform?

5. Are you willing to say "no" to PRs, issues, and forks you disagree with? Kind physicians make bad BDFLs.

Decision questions on GitHub

faqs.title: What are the dominant failure modes, and how does governance mitigate them?

items.question: What does the OSEHRA cautionary tale teach?
items.answer_html:
OSEHRA (Open Source Electronic Health Record Alliance) had 850+ members and collapsed in 2020 when the VA — the anchor tenant — left for Cerner. The lesson: single-sponsor OS in healthcare dies when the sponsor loses interest.

For Meridian, this means: don't open-source until there's at least one non-Optum adopter. A second site is the minimum viable signal that the project can survive a sponsor exit.

OSEHRA reference on GitHub

items.question: How real is maintainer burnout?
items.answer_html:
Very real. Linux Foundation 2024 OSPO data: 60% of maintainers are unpaid. "Doctors Who Code" explicitly warns of the stall-guilt-abandon arc for clinician developers. Issues, security disclosures, CVE responses become unpaid second-job work unless staffed.

Mitigation: a clearly-scoped project with a documented "maintainers respond within 2 weeks, contributions via PR only" contract is sustainable. You don't owe OSS your nights and weekends. The scope constraint matters more than the license choice.

items.question: What governance model works for a clinician-led project?
items.answer_html:
Apache 2.0 + a published GOVERNANCE.md with clear rules:

Maintainers have final say — roadmap owned by Dr. Schmuckler.
Contributions via PR only — no direct commits from external contributors.
Maintainers respond within 2 weeks — sustainable cadence, not a second job.
Clinical content requires physician sign-off — non-negotiable for a clinical tool.

The projects that lose direction are the ones that don't write this down. Direction drift is real but manageable via governance. The 90% solution is documenting the norms.
